does: This paper constructs simplified mathematical models of neural networks that exhibit polysemanticity—the phenomenon where individual neurons respond to multiple unrelated concepts rather than single, interpretable features. By building tractable toy models, the authors can fully trace how and why polysemanticity emerges during training. They analyze the conditions under which networks compress information by reusing neurons for multiple features, studying the trade-offs between model capacity, sparsity of feature activations, and the pressure to represent high-dimensional data in limited neurons. The work provides concrete mathematical understanding of polysemanticity's origins through controlled experiments where all components are visible and measurable.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of neural network interpretability research grappling with why real networks develop seemingly uninterpretable neural representations. It extends prior mechanistic interpretability efforts that attempt to reverse-engineer learned circuits and feature detectors, responding to the puzzle that emerged from studies of vision and language models where neurons stubbornly represent mixed concepts. The paper connects to classical information theory and dimensionality reduction literature, combining insights about compression and sparse coding with modern concerns about neural network interpretability. It also relates to superposition theory in physics and mathematics, applying these concepts to understand how networks can encode more features than they have neurons.
enables: By demonstrating that polysemanticity is a rational consequence of training under realistic constraints rather than a mysterious anomaly, this work opens paths toward understanding and potentially controlling feature emergence in larger models. The toy models provide a testbed for developing better interpretability techniques that must contend with superposed features, potentially leading to methods that can unpack mixed representations. This foundation supports future work on identifying when polysemanticity becomes problematic for alignment and transparency, how to design architectures that avoid it if needed, and whether techniques like sparse autoencoders or other decomposition methods can reliably disentangle superposed features. It also suggests that interpretability tools must account for systematic feature mixing rather than assuming clean one-neuron-one-concept mappings.
